issue_title: Convert unicode URLs to punycode
issue_body:
I noticed that furl doesn't like to handle unicode URLs. Would it make sense to make furl convert passed in unicode hostnames to punycode? It would make it a lot easier to handle user input. Using the [idna](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/idna) lib makes this trivial, and would be nice to have built in.

Here's how to do it (in Python 3.5):

```python
>>> url = 'http://åäö.se/'
>>> import idna, furl
>>> from urllib.parse import urlparse
>>> parsed = urlparse(url)
>>> parsed = parsed._replace(netloc=idna.encode(parsed.netloc).decode())
>>> punyfied = furl.furl(parsed.geturl())
>>> print(punyfied)
http://xn--4cab6c.se/
```

issue_title: Str representation of mpq is different from Fractions
issue_body:
Hi, with the following example you can see a discrepancy between the behavior of ```str``` of mpq and Fractions in python 2.7 (I tried on 2.7.9). The difference is the trailing L at the end of the numerator:

```
import gmpy2
a = gmpy2.mpq(2**200, 7)
print(str(a))
# '1606938044258990275541962092341162602522202993782792835301376L/7'
from fractions import Fraction
b = Fraction(2**200, 7)
print(str(b))
# '1606938044258990275541962092341162602522202993782792835301376/7'
```

This seems to be controlled by an explicit condition in the code testing for PY2 (see [1]). I think that this has to do with the difference between ```repr``` and ```str``` in py2, as discussed in [2].

[1] https://github.com/aleaxit/gmpy/blob/8a5de1d3c32ba9f7e5e7c873d5ff44fda46ba561/src/gmpy2_convert_gmp.c#L962

[2] http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24059842/l-suffix-in-long-integer-in-python-3-x

issue_title: Should we provide Python 2.5 and older Python support in dpkt 2.0?
issue_body:
Hi there,

Recently I was working on migrate `dpkt` to Python 3, which is an important feature for dpkt 2.0. I discussed with @kbandla early today about dropping the support for Python 2.5 and older versions in our new release. Because we suppose there are not so many users still run our packet on those old Python machines, also it will make it easier for our migration job. For instance, in Python 2 iterators have a `.next()` method we use to get the next value from the iterator. For example,

``` python
>>> i = iter(range(5))
>>> i.next()
0
>>> i.next()
1
```

This special method has in Python 3 been renamed to `.__next__()` to be consistent with the naming of special attributes elswhere in Python. However, we should generally not call it directly, but instead use the builtin is `next()` function. _However, this function is only available from Python 2.6._

There are also some similar issues for the migration to Python 3. Thus what's your opinion? Should we just drop the support for old Python versions (<= 2.5)?

Thanks,
Hao

issue_title: Polling for interest in adding variable body length strictness in HTTP parsing
IEEE80211 Prints error directly to CLI
issue_body:
In the IEEE80211 class's `unpack` method on line [418](https://github.com/kbandla/dpkt/blob/2208e638f8f3939ba877a595ce75925710500a44/dpkt/ieee80211.py#L418). There is a print statement exposing the error directly to CLI. 

This is a problem if one is to use DPKT in combination with for instance [curses](https://docs.python.org/2/howto/curses.html), as it disrupts the UI of the developed application.  Therefore we suggest removing this print statement and instead return an error code of some sort. 

EDIT: 
From what I can tell you could either just let the developer handle the exception self, or simply delete the print statement

issue_title: weather.py fails when forecast has a wind response by no value (NonType has no copy fcn)
zenpy re-mounting the https transport breaks requests_toolbelt's monkeypatch for Google Appengine
issue_body:
Apparently zenpy remounts the HTTPS transport on whatever requests session it gets:

From https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/__init__.py#L46

```python
# Workaround for https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/2364
session.mount('https://', HTTPAdapter(max_retries=3))
```

This prevents the correct working on Google Appengine, even if requests_toolbelt monkeypatch is used (see https://toolbelt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/adapters.html#appengineadapter). This in turn results in a lot of connection errors when running in Google Appengine standard environments:

```
*** ConnectionError: ConnectionError(MaxRetryError("HTTPSConnectionPool(host='[redacted]', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /api/v2/search.json[redacted] (Caused by ProtocolError('Connection aborted.', error(13, 'Permission denied')))",),)
```

The workaround is to redefine what HTTPAdapter is for zenpy after importing it.

```python
zenpy.HTTPAdapter = requests_toolbelt.adapters.appengine.AppEngineAdapter
```

issue_title: pip install fails when installing libraw from source
issue_body:
It happens when you installed libraw from source and there is no package installed.

Error message:
```
libraw_version.h: No such file or directory
```

I have fixed it with following patch, but we should find better solution to merge into master.

```diff
diff --git a/setup.py b/setup.py
index 63e1a09..ae20b3d 100644
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -103,6 +103,11 @@ if isWindows or isMac:
     libraw_config_found = True
 else:
     use_pkg_config()
+
+    include_dirs += ['/usr/local/include/libraw']
+    library_dirs += ['/usr/local/lib']
+    libraries = ['raw_r']
+    libraw_config_found = True
     
     # check if libraw_config.h exists
     # this header is only installed when using cmake
```

issue_title: Search criteria starting with a wildcard returns no results
issue_body:
Using Python 2.7.13 and ldap3 3.2.  Backend LDAP is OpenDJ 3.5.

A search filter with only a wildcard or a wildcard at the end returns entries, but a filter with a wildcard at the start will not.  

(uid=PERKINS13)  will correctly return one entry
(uid=PERKINS*) will correctly return multiple entries
(uid=*ERKINS13) will incorrectly return no entries

I have confirmed using red hat command line ldapsearch will return entries correctly on all 3 cases.

issue_title: Performance degradation in 2.x release

PYTHONPATH=.eggs/ldap3-2.0.9-py3.6.egg:.eggs/pyasn1-0.1.9-py3.6.egg python3 -  1.21s user 0.51s system 42% cpu 4.068 total
$ hg update master
$ time PYTHONPATH=.eggs/ldap3-1.4.0-py3.6.egg:.eggs/pyasn1-0.1.9-py3.6.egg python -m yg.ldap.commands --username ygapp_ygldap --password $YGAPP_YG_LDAP_PASSWORD jason
...
PYTHONPATH=.eggs/ldap3-1.4.0-py3.6.egg:.eggs/pyasn1-0.1.9-py3.6.egg python3 -  0.76s user 0.12s system 47% cpu 1.835 total
```

The only differences in the code between master and ldap3-2 are the compatibility changes. As you can see, the same operation takes almost twice as long. And if I run a full test suite, where the ldap3 2.x changes dominate the behavior, the factor is more noticeable.

Are there any changes in version 2 that are expected to impact performance? Have you benchmarked the behavior in any other environments? I expect the inferred schema for attributes is probably more expensive. Do you have any other advice for improving performance?

Again, it's possible the degradation is a non-issue, but I wanted to report my findings here and get any advice you might have.

issue_title: Searching for SINGLE-VALUE attribute with no values returns empty list
issue_body:
If one were to perform a search and explicitly request an attribute that is SINGLE-VALUE according to the schema then the returned value is a single value if the attribute is set on the object but an empty list if the attribute is missing.
I would argue that None would be a more sensible value than an empty list.

```python
conn.search("dc=internal,dc=machines", "(cn=mattias.testgroup)", ldap3.SUBTREE, attributes=["single_value_test_attr"])
```
Response when attribute exists on the object:
```
[{
  'raw_dn': b'cn=mattias.testgroup,ou=Groups,dc=internal,dc=machines',
  'dn': 'cn=mattias.testgroup,ou=Groups,dc=internal,dc=machines',
  'raw_attributes': {'single_value_test_attr': [b'abc']},
  'attributes': {'single_value_test_attr': 'abc'},
  'type': 'searchResEntry'
}]
```
Result when missing:
```
[{
  'raw_dn': b'cn=mattias.testgroup,ou=Groups,dc=internal,dc=machines',
  'dn': 'cn=mattias.testgroup,ou=Groups,dc=internal,dc=machines',
  'raw_attributes': {'single_value_test_attr': []},
  'attributes': {'single_value_test_attr': []},
  'type': 'searchResEntry'
}]
```

Unless this is intended behaviour, replacing [this line](https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/blob/dev/ldap3/strategy/base.py#L407) with something like the code below seems to correct this

```python
if self.connection.server.schema and attribute_type in self.connection.server.schema.attribute_types and self.connection.server.schema.attribute_types[attribute_type].single_value:
    entry['attributes'][attribute_type] = None
else:
    entry['attributes'][attribute_type] = list()
```
Response after change when attribute is missing:
```
[{
  'raw_dn': b'cn=mattias.testgroup,ou=Groups,dc=internal,dc=machines',
  'dn': 'cn=mattias.testgroup,ou=Groups,dc=internal,dc=machines',
  'raw_attributes': {'single_value_test_attr': []},
  'attributes': {'single_value_test_attr': None},
  'type': 'searchResEntry'
}]
```

issue_title: Support computation and validation of OIDC at_hash value
issue_body:
Open ID Connect specifies an optional additional claim for its ID token JWTs: the [`at_hash`](http://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#CodeIDToken).
It's just a hash of the access token issued alongside the ID token.

Although OIDC lists it as optional, it also states that it's required when performing the "Authorization Code Grant" (3-legged OAuth), which is confusing.

It's very easy to compute. Just
```python
base64.urlsafe_b64encode(
    hashlib.sha256(access_token).digest()[:128]
    ).rstrip('=')
```

replacing `hashlib.sha256` with whatever `alg` is specified.

For those implementing OIDC servers, and clients of those servers, computing and validating this `at_hash` value, respectively, makes sense as PyJWT functionality.

issue_title: Very small triangles pass through merge_vertices but cause issues in normal computation
issue_body:
When using `triangles.normals()` the cross product of two sides of the triangle is first calculated, then unitized in `util.unitize()`.

When calculating this cross product, it is possible for vertices that are too far apart to be merged in preprocessing under the standard 1e-8 `TOL_MERGE` to result in sides with a cross product with norm  smaller than the 1e-13 `TOL_ZERO` specified in util.py

For example, a triangle with all sides having length 1.1e-8 will not be merged, but the cross product of two sides has length 1.1e-8 ^ 2 * sin (pi/3) = 1.0e-16, which is zero according to TOL_ZERO.

This causes `triangles.normals()` to deem the face invalid even though it passes through `base.merge_vertices()`.

This is mostly a problem in `apply_transform`, where `normals_pre` and `normals_post` can be invalid if the first face falls under this condition, which breaks `triangles.windings_aligned()` and causes an exception.

issue_title: Design question for RFQ/non-continuous markets
issue_body:
Congrats on you work. 

Just pondering how this would be used in R.F.Q. style markets where we only have sporadic buying and selling opportunities and where we compete with other dealers to win a customer order. Supposing we have a trade record of the winning response price/size, I *think* I would do something like the following:

  1. Write  class MaybeBuy(Algo):  to return True only when there is a buying opportunity. Similarly for sell opportunities. 

   2. Write another "Algo" to return suggested bid/offer that fires when MaybeBuy is True 

   3. Write another Algo   WinRFQ that returns True if our price is the best. 

[Perhaps combine 1.2.3]

   4. Write a Strategy that has member class commision_fn  to mock the actual economics of buying/selling at the price where we won the auction. This would also "allocate" an amount equal to the trade size. 

Can you think of anything I'm missing?

issue_title: Absolute path to target directory on Windows interpreted as remote target
issue_body:
With this command 
```
pip2pi C:\HTdocs\Fileserver\Programming\Python\pypi_pkgs -n pep8
```

pip2pi interprets the target as remote target and tries to use rsync which fails. The problem is how a remote target is identified, namely by `if ':' in target:`. I solved this by changing the line to `if re.match('.*\..*:', target):`. Since I do not know much about regular expressions, I am not sure if this is a good solution.

Another point: Since rsync is not available on Windows by default, a note about rsync in the requirements section in readme.rst would be helpful.

issue_title: Feature Request
issue_body:
etmtk 3.2.27 on linux 4.5.4

Currently, when one schedules a group task and sets an alarm, the alarm reminder box is triggered for ALL sub-tasks; so that if one has 6 sub-tasks, each a separate job, one gets six alert reminder boxes all at once.

Request is that each @j is addressed separately so that a reminder or alarm @a can be set for each sub-task individually.

issue_title: ValueError: No such option 'max-line-length'
issue_body:
The documentation lists options for the CLI side of `autopep8`, but not for using it as a module. It's logical therefore to assume that all CLI options that make sense as module options would also be module options, but that does not seem to be the case.

My code:
```
data.write(autopep8.fix_code(output,

The result:

    options = _get_options(options, apply_config)

issue_title: Cannot share local and remote targets for both upload and download synchronizers
issue_body:
If I create:
```python
local_target = FsTarget("/path/to/local/dir")
remote_target = FtpTarget(path="/path/to/remote/dir", host="ftp.example.com")
```
then use these targets for both an upload and a download synchronizer:
```python
ftp_downloader = DownloadSynchronizer(local_target, remote_target)
ftp_uploader = UploadSynchronizer(local_target, remote_target)
ftp_downloader.run()
ftp_uploader.run()
```
this leads to the following error on the `ftp_uploader.run()` line:
```
RuntimeError: target is read-only: <ftp:ftp.example.com/path/to/remote/dir + .>
```
This is because the constructors for `DownloadSynchronizer` and `UploadSynchronizer` set the remote/local targets as `readonly`, respectively, without making a copy of the provided object. A simple hack to fix this is to explicitly set the local/remote targets in this case to non-readonly in between:
```python
ftp_downloader = DownloadSynchronizer(local_target, remote_target)
ftp_uploader = UploadSynchronizer(local_target, remote_target)
ftp_downloader.run()
# HACK: set remote to non-readonly
remote_target.readonly = False
ftp_uploader.run()
```
It would probably be better if the constructor explicitly sets the read/write status of *both* the remote and local targets. For example, the `DownloadSynchronizer` would have:
```python
def __init__(self, local, remote, options):
    super(DownloadSynchronizer, self).__init__(local, remote, options)
    local.readonly = False
    remote.readonly = True
```

issue_title: Create a unified tech stack for jedi, rope, RedBaron, yapf, pylint, pep8, and similar tools and libraries
issue_body:
Currently there exists duplication of effort around tooling for parsing, automated manipulation, and analyzing Python code. Each project in this space faces similar fundamental problems of requiring a data representation of the Python code of interest, most commonly represented as a syntax tree. Here is a list, by no means exhaustive, of tools and libraries that face the problem of needing a representation of Python code capable of inspection and possibly even manipulation:
- Python standard library's [`ast`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ast.html#module-ast)
- Python standard library's [`lib2to3`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/2to3.html?highlight=2to3#module-lib2to3)
- Python standard library's [`2to3` tool](https://docs.python.org/3/library/2to3.html)
- [jedi](https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi)
- [rope](https://github.com/python-rope/rope/)
- [pep8](https://github.com/PyCQA/pep8)
- [astroid](https://github.com/landscapeio/astroid) and [pylint](http://www.pylint.org/)
- [yapf](https://github.com/google/yapf)
- [baron](https://github.com/Psycojoker/baron), [redbaron](https://github.com/Psycojoker/redbaron), and [pyfmt](https://github.com/Psycojoker/pyfmt)

The overlap between any of these projects is by no means complete, but the overlap between all of them is still significant, especially given they solve the same underlying problem of Python code representation. My hope here is we can examine whether the Python ecosystem really needs this many implementations of the Python syntax tree, and whether we can arrive at an ecosystem that has a unified base, with the diversity of effort occurring on more interesting problem spaces such as advanced refactoring and static analyses.

I am hoping to start the discussion here in jedi's GitHub Issues because of the project's recent announcement of a [representation of Python code based upon an (improved?) fork of `lib2to3`](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/code-quality/2015-April/000534.html), which, from my understanding should provide a usable syntax tree representation for both Python 2 and Python 3.

One question is whether the jedi library's API is wide enough to support projects beyond jedi, and, if it's not, what additions must be made to make it more universal? We must acknowledge that each tool and library mentioned in the list above may only need some portion of information from the syntax tree, but that a good underlying representation would have the union of such information required by all client tools and libraries. Clearly some requirements gathering must be done, but my hope is there is a strong and reconcilable overlap in requirements between the projects.

Another question is, should this library be broken out into its own Python distribution package? This would allow the fundamental underlying library to move at its own pace independent of jedi's release schedule. This means jedi would become a client of library, as I hope other libraries and tools could.

It also seems that the base representation, in being an _accurate_ representation of Python code, may not be the most _convenient_ or appropriate interface through which a client should interact with the code, suggesting room for higher level APIs, provided by separate libraries that wrap the base representation, for example, how [pandas](http://pandas.pydata.org/) is to [numpy](http://www.numpy.org/). I think there exists the opportunity to create a library stack, in which the tools mentioned above become clients of the high-level libraries mentioned above, which in turn are clients of the base representation library. In fact, we can see discussion of these very ideas already within and between multiple projects, e.g., python-rope/rope#57, and Psycojoker/baron#61. I think now is the time to get serious about these efforts.

Finally, I'd like to point to two inspirations that this collaboration of effort and unification can happen. The first inspiration is the scientific Python community's unification around the HDF5 Python stack, which I encourage you to read about in @scopatz's [excellent blog post about that effort](https://hdfgroup.org/wp/2015/09/python-hdf5-a-vision/). The second inspiration comes from @royrapoport's description of the way Netflix works in [his recent interview on Talk Python To Me](http://talkpython.fm/episodes/show/16/python-at-netflix). There, teams are permitted to make their own engineering decisions, but this inevitably creates duplication of effort. To counteract that duplication, Netflix periodically evaluates overlapping projects, and if their duplication is found technically unjustifiable, the project's maintainers must sit down with each other and figure out how to merge the code bases. While there's no fiduciary incentive in the case of the projects I've mentioned above here, there is still the incentive of reducing the amount of time, personally and collectively, spent solving very similar problems.

I think a similar effort here to unify would be a great win for Python tooling and the Python community. My hope is this opens up the conversation between these various projects.

cc @davidhalter @aligrudi @mcepl @Psycojoker @gwelymernans @hayd @jcrocholl @florentx @IanLee1521 @PCManticore

I will also try to reach out to maintainers of other projects whom I could not find on GitHub, but if anyone here has contacts mentioned or omitted but interested, please reach out to them as well.

@davidhalter, forgive me for hijacking your project's Issues for this.

Apologies also for anyone who is offended this discussion would happen on GitHub or in a GitHub issue. To me this seems the most open, publicly visible, conducive space to begin the conversation, though, as I suspect few people are members of the [Python code-quality mailing list](https://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/code-quality) when compared to the number of people impacted and interested.

issue_title: When the number of processes comes to 3000+, psutil will consume more CPU resource.
issue_body:
Hi!

Recently I find a problem when I use psutil to enumerate the processes in my system and print the detail of them. The problem is that the module psutil consumes more CPU resource when the number of processes  comes to 3000+. It is about 40% on one core!

Here is my code.

```python
def getprocstatus():
    '''
    Returns:
        List of the status of the processes
    Exception:
        For Exception Location
    '''
    PROCTARGET = []
    for pid in psutil.pids():
        try:
            # intialization
            procName = ""
            procExe = ""
            procCwd = ""
            proccmd = ""
            procConn = []
            procUser = ""
            procCrttime = 0

            # setting value
            proc = psutil.Process(pid)
            procName = proc.name()
            procExe = proc.exe()
            procCwd = proc.cwd()
            procCmd = proc.cmdline()
            for cmd in procCmd:
                proccmd += cmd
                proccmd += " "
            proccmd = proccmd.strip()
            procConn = getLISTENConn(proc.connections())
            procUser = proc.username()
            procCrttime = int(proc.create_time())
            tupple = (pid,procName,procExe,procCwd,proccmd,procConn,procUser,procCrttime)
            PROCTARGET.append(tupple)
        except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
            mystr = "getprocstatus: NoSuchProcess exception pid=%d" % pid
            logtofile(RUNLOGFILE,mystr,writeflag=False)
        except Exception, e:
            mystr="getprocstatus: exception %s" %(traceback.format_exc().replace('\n',' '))
            logtofile(RUNLOGFILE,mystr)

    return PROCTARGET
```

When python runs to code of  `proc = psutil.Process(pid)`, it slows down and consume lots of CPU resource.

Environment below:
```bash
ps -ef |wc -l
3926
```

Result:
```bash
ps aux| grep procstatus.py |grep -v grep
root     117983 94.3  0.2 208392 20104 pts/2    R+   14:03   0:05 /LAPPTIV/upshield/bin/python mylib/procstatus.py
```

It means that at that moment the CPU consume of the process of procstatus is up to 94.3%!

SO what can I do for this problem?

issue_title: Every season premiere could take up to 3 weeks to get subs using legendastv provider
issue_body:
For the latest subliminal version (2.0.5) or latest development version

Given any Series that has a season premiere (e.g: Season 3, episode 1) today/tonight, subliminal can take up to 3 weeks to download subtitles for the new episodes in the new season.

This part:
https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/blob/master/subliminal/providers/legendastv.py#L202-L203

searches for titles with a given name. In case of tv shows, each item of the returned list represents a "Season" for the given series. E.g.:
- My Show Season 1
- My Show Season 2

Since the returned list is cached, I noticed a delay of weeks for subliminal to identify a new "season entry" in that list and start searching for subtitles for the new episodes.

That method searches legendastv website using the very same endpoint that the auto suggestion input field "Procurar" ("Search" in English) in the right corner of the home page uses ( http://legendas.tv/ ). Every user that visits the home page and start typing, is actually hitting that endpoint. Its performance and response time is considerable good.

In my point of view, either the cache expiration time should be reduced or it should be dropped for that method.

issue_title: [RFE] Configurable list of excluded keys from the cooldown check
issue_body:
In the current state, the [cooldown](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/async/discord/ext/commands/cooldowns.py) functionality does not allow excluding certain keys (users/roles/etc.) from the cooldown check - e.g. when having a cooldown check with `BucketType.user` type[0], it would be nice to have a list of users for which this check would be always skipped (regular users vs admins).

The first solution which came to my mind would be creating a dummy class, which would inherit properties of the `Cooldown` class, but the `is_rate_limited()` method would always return zero. Then the `CooldownMapping.get_bucket()` would instantiate this class instead of the `Cooldown` one for all keys from the exclusion list.

But of course there are definitely better solutions to achieve this.

[0] `@commands.cooldown(rate=3, per=60, type=commands.BucketType.user)`

...

The generated ymal looks like this:

      array_field:

issue_title: KIP-74 / KAFKA-2063: max_partition_fetch_bytes is now a soft limit
issue_body:
I noticed that Kafka consumer config docs for the `max_partition_fetch_bytes` param now specify that it's a soft limit so that the consumer can make progress. 

It looks like the change happened as part of [KIP-74](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-74%3A+Add+Fetch+Response+Size+Limit+in+Bytes) / [KAFKA-2063](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/KAFKA-2063) / [actual implementation](https://github.com/apache/kafka/pull/1812), which happened in Kafka 0.10.1.

This was further amended in Kafka 0.11 as part of [KAFKA-5032](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/KAFKA-5032) which relates to the new V2 message format which @tvoinarovskyi is working on in https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/pull/1185.

Here's some of the relevant code in `kafka-python` that will need to be updated:
* https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/fc9da053c8656c2d846889b1c08d54cecd3907d0/kafka/consumer/fetcher.py#L83-L87
* https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/fc9da053c8656c2d846889b1c08d54cecd3907d0/kafka/consumer/fetcher.py#L328-L347

PROTOCOLS = ['h2', 'spdy/3', 'http/1.1']

def supports_alpn():
	try:
		ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
		ctx.check_hostname = False
		ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
		ctx.set_alpn_protocols(PROTOCOLS)

		connection = ctx.wrap_socket(
		   socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM), server_hostname=HOST)

		connection.connect((HOST, PORT))

		return connection.selected_alpn_protocol() != None

		#TODO : Connection close

	except:
		pass

	return False


def supports_npn():
	try:
		ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
		ctx.check_hostname = False
		ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
		ctx.set_npn_protocols(PROTOCOLS)

		connection = ctx.wrap_socket(
		   socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM), server_hostname=HOST)

		connection.connect((HOST, PORT))

		return connection.selected_npn_protocol() != None

		#TODO : Connection close

	except:
		pass

	return False
```

issue_title: Improper bytes/unicode in glances_hddtemp.py
issue_body:
Hello,

with python3.4 and hddtemp enabled, i can't get access to the web interface.

Digging in the HTTP traffic shows an API call to api/2/all that fails, with a JSON encoding error (choking on bytes data).

Indeed `glances_hddtemp.py` seems to have an encoding problem with the temperature value.

In `GlancesGrabHDDTemp.__update__`:

`temperature = fields[offset + 3]` means that temperature has 'bytes' type

then `hddtemp_current['value'] = float(temperature) if isinstance(temperature, numbers.Number) else temperature` ==> `if isinstance(temperature, numbers.Number)` is always False ==> `hddtemp_current['value']` has also 'bytes' type

instead, maybe do:

```python
try:
    hddtemp_current['value'] = float(temperature)
except ValueError:
    hddtemp_current['value'] = nativestr(temperature)
```
Hope it helps :)

issue_title: (Terminal) BELLs for critical errors
issue_body:
**Description**

One of the more interesting functions of the Glances is the ability to detect and show a critical status (when some value is too high or not working correctly), this helps a lot! But sometimes we are not seeing the terminal where the Glances is opened and cannot able to see a critical problem in time.

So I was thinking is could be interesting add some audio support to it. But theres many people that uses Glances remotely (me for example) and pass a sound alert could be some difficult, but I found a way, a terminal bell!

I know a terminal beep is somewhat boring but can be very helpful in cases that the terminal is out of the view. So is possible to us to add some optional "Terminal Beep" support to Glances.

The idea is that beep alert be disable by default and can be enable by user via command line parameter (--enable-bell) and via glances.conf and via key shortcut.

The terminal bell support is relative easy to implement, is just need to send a '\a' to the terminal, this can be done using a print('\a') or sys.stdout.write('\a').

I think would be cool to implement in Glances to send a bell per refresh when a critical alert is happening this way.

What do you think that idea?

Sorry for any bad english.

issue_title: Allow nullable values
issue_body:
I would like to add a way to validate nullable values.  I thought that the format validation might allow me to do this, but it is separate from the type validation.   I don't mind submitting the code, but I would love a pointer on where to start.  

The biggest potential issue is that swagger does not officially define nullable fields. In fact, `null` isn't allowed in the spec. So either a spec would need to be picked or it would need to be configurable. We have been using `x-nullable: true` in our specs.   For example,

```yaml
properties:
  id:
    type: integer
    format: int32
  organization_id:
    type: integer
    format: int32
  email:
    type: string
  first_name:
    type: string
    x-nullable: true
```

As per https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/issues/229, `x-nullable` and `x-isnullable` seem to be popular choices for people with nullable fields.

issue_title: Detecting different message in similar Exception objects
issue_body:
Hi,

First of all, thanks for writing a great library. I have used it in a lot of testing and it saved me from so many bugs.

I just ran into a small oddity; it seems that when comparing Exception objects the 'message' attribute is not checked for differences. Consider this snippet:

```python
>>> from deepdiff import DeepDiff
>>>
>>> e1 = KeyError()
>>> e2 = KeyError('foo')
>>> class ClassA(object):
...   a = 1
...   def __init__(self, b):
...     self.b = b
...
>>> t1 = ClassA('')
>>> t2 = ClassA('foo')
>>> DeepDiff(e1, e2, verbose_level=2)
{}
>>> DeepDiff(t1, t2, verbose_level=2)
{'values_changed': {'root.b': {'new_value': 'foo', 'old_value': ''}}}
>>> e1.message
''
>>> e2.message
'foo'
>>> t1.b
''
>>> t2.b
'foo'
```

Tested with DeepDiff 3.3.0, same behavior on Python 2.7 and 3.6

I noticed [this PR ](https://github.com/seperman/deepdiff/pull/75) should theoretically have fixed this, as the message attribute is in fact returned by `dir()`:
```python
>>> {i: getattr(e1, i) for i in dir(e1) if not (i.startswith('__') and i.endswith('__'))}
{'message': '', 'args': ()}
>>> {i: getattr(e2, i) for i in dir(e2) if not (i.startswith('__') and i.endswith('__'))}
{'message': 'foo', 'args': ('foo',)}
```

But apparently it doesn't, so perhaps we don't get into __search_obj at all, or something's off in __search_dict... Debugging is fun, but don't have a lot of time for it now. Just posting the issue here and will check it later. :)

Cheers,
Mark

issue_title: ThemedStyle.theme_use doesn't work without arguments
issue_body:
`ttk.Style().theme_use()` can be called with no arguments to check which ttk theme is currently being used, but `ttkthemes.ThemedStyle` doesn't support it.

```python
>>> from tkinter import ttk
>>> import ttkthemes
>>> ttk.Style().theme_use()
'default'
>>> ttkthemes.ThemedStyle().theme_use()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
TypeError: set_theme() missing 1 required positional argument: 'theme_name'
>>>
```

Of course, this is easy to fix with e.g. `style.tk.eval("return $ttk::currentTheme")` (yes, I got this from tkinter's code), but it would be nice if the `theme_use` method was compatible with the `theme_use` it overrides (instead of just aliasing it to `set_theme`).

issue_title: generate from list of weighted terms instead of full text only
issue_body:
The **wordcloud_cli.py** could be extended to read from a weighted term list instead of full text only. It makes sense if one knows the terms and their weights beforehand. 

Example:
```
wordcloud_cli.py --imagefile foo.png --text terms.txt --csv
```
**terms.txt** would list the terms and their weights:
```
term, weight
term1, 2
term two, 1
...
term n, 3
```

**Workaround**:
```
def main(args):
    wordcloud = wc.WordCloud(stopwords=args.stopwords, mask=args.mask,
        width=args.width, height=args.height, font_path=args.font_path,
        margin=args.margin, relative_scaling=args.relative_scaling,
        color_func=args.color_func, background_color=args.background_color)
    if not args.csv:
        wordcloud = wordcloud.generate(args.text)
    else:
        wordcloud = wordcloud.generate_from_frequencies(args.text)
...
```

```

def parse_args(arguments):
...
    parser.add_argument('--csv', default=False, action='store_true',
        help='indicates text file contains CSV of weighted terms; 1st column: terms, 2nd colum: weight as integer, 1st row: column names')
...
    if not args.csv:
        with args.text:
            args.text = args.text.read()
    else:
        reader = csv.DictReader(args.text)
        args.text = list()
        for row in reader:
            args.text.append((row[reader.fieldnames[0]], int(row[reader.fieldnames[1]])))
...
```

I would classify the issue's priority as low. In case one finds this feature request irrElephant, simply close this issue.

tnx,
r²

issue_title: Reverse Issue 5: Python 2.7 input/output should really be strings and not Unicode
issue_body:
Reverse the behavior change per Issue 5.

For Python 2.7, input/output should really be strings and not Unicode.
That should be the most expected behavior for most users.

But keep the behavior as it is for Python 3, as in Python 3 all strings are Unicode.

Originally opened 2014-11-21. 
This issue was ported from Bitbucket and is archived for historical reasons.

issue_title: stream the response rather than buffering it in memory
issue_body: Arbitrarily large objects will cause memory problems if it's all buffered. Switch to reading and responding with a line at a time with chunked transfer encoding on the response.

issue_title: use WSGIContext
issue_body: Use the swift.common.wsgi.WSGIContext helper class to ensure that the response is ready for you to consume before actually consuming it. See the catch_errors middleware for a simple example. staticweb is a more complex example.

issue_title: Negative numbers return the wrong mode
issue_body:
Release 1.1 introduced a bug:
<https://github.com/mnooner256/pyqrcode/blob/master/pyqrcode/__init__.py#L280>

Values like '-1' or -1 are valid integers but aren't supported by "numeric" mode. The encoder must use the "alphanumeric" mode.

issue_title: Get rid of defunct processes when using honcho in a docker context
issue_body:
I use honcho as entrypoint in a docker context to manage multi-services docker container.
It works fine, however [in some cases of use, it creates defunct processes](https://blog.phusion.nl/2015/01/20/docker-and-the-pid-1-zombie-reaping-problem/).

To get rid of that, I modified the method Manager.loop to act as init, for waiting zombie processes to terminate, if honcho has pid 1.

Nota : I used an old version of psutil (0.6.1) module, because I am working on RHEL6 and I used standard rpm package.

Here is the diff patch :

```
--- manager.py  2016-09-28 15:56:52.781413761 +0200
+++ ../../../poc_with_barman/build/manager.py   2016-09-28 13:47:20.207242021 +0200
@@ -1,3 +1,6 @@
+import psutil
+import os
+
 import datetime
 import multiprocessing
 import signal
@@ -97,6 +100,21 @@
             self.returncode = SIGNALS[signum]['rc']
             self.terminate()

+        """
+        If pid=1 honcho act as init processus and must wait for orphean
+        process to avoid zombie.
+        This is usefull when you use honcho as entrypoint in docker image.
+        """
+        def _wait_zombie():
+                if os.getpid() == 1:
+                        procs = []
+                        for proc in psutil.process_iter():
+                                try:
+                                        if proc.status == psutil.STATUS_ZOMBIE:
+                                                proc.wait(timeout=KILL_WAIT)
+                                except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
+                                        pass
+
         signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _terminate)
         signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, _terminate)

@@ -139,6 +157,8 @@
                 if waiting > datetime.timedelta(seconds=KILL_WAIT):
                     self.kill()

+            _wait_zombie()
+
     def terminate(self):
         """
         Terminate all processes managed by this ProcessManager.
```

Hi,

What is current version: 2.0.0 or 1.4.2?

issue_title: bumpversion and setuptools have differing interpolation behavior
issue_body:
First, thanks for the great tool!

The interpolation behavior of config files by `bumpversion` and `setuptools` is not the same. `bumpversion` uses no interpolation (e.g. [`configparser.RawConfigParser`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html#configparser.RawConfigParser)), but `setuptools` uses the default interpolation of [`configparser.ConfigParser`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html#configparser.ConfigParser), which is "basic interpolation".

In general, this should not be an issue, but because `bumpversion` supports setting configuration in the `setup.cfg` file, options that might be interpolated by the parser would not cause an issue if they appear in the `.bumpversion.cfg`, but suddenly break the install/build process if they are moved to the `setup.cfg`.

For example, I had the following in my `.bumpversion.cfg` to automatically insert the date in my CHANGELOG along with the version.

```
[bumpversion:file:docs/source/changelog.rst]
search = XX-XX-XXXX v. X.X.X
replace = {now:%m-%d-%Y} v. {new_version}
```

When I moved this into my `setup.cfg`, `bumpversion` still works but the following happens when I try to run anything with `setup.py`.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
 ...
configparser.InterpolationSyntaxError: '%' must be followed by '%' or '(', found: '%m-%d-%Y} v. {new_version}'
```

The obvious solution of escaping the `%` so that they are not interpolated (e.g. `{now:%%m-%%d-%%Y}` causes `setuptools` to not fail, but then `bumpversion` inserts the literal string "`%%m-%%d-%%Y`" instead of formatting the date because the percent signs are not treated as needing escaping.

It turns out that other packages have run into this, like `pytest`: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/3062. [This `distutils` mailing thread](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/distutils-sig/2017-July/031091.html) also illustrates that the python devs are struggling with if this was a good design decision, but that's a whole other can of worms.

**My proposal**: When loading from `setup.cfg`, use `configparser.ConfigParser`, and when loading from `.bumpversion.cfg`, use `configparser.RawConfigParser`.

issue_title: Add support for -r inclusion
issue_body:
"dev" requirements typically also load the install requirements via ``-r requirements.txt`` – load and expand those lines as-if the inclusion appeared at that place. This has to work recursively. 

Best implemented as a pre-processor that resolves any "-r" line until none are there any more, and THEN parses the list of requirements.

issue_title: LocalMachine is not parallel-safe (threadsafe)
issue_body:
After following up on #168, I saw that `local` is an instance of `plumbum.machines.local.LocalMachine`. As [stated in the docstring, however, `LocalMachine` was designed to be a singleton](https://github.com/tomerfiliba/plumbum/blob/9cfee6125c2d541ec423e1af8976da02fcc78220/plumbum/machines/local.py#L109). Because new instances of `LocalMachine` would just use the same class attributes for `cwd`, `env`, `python` and the like, it seems that this class is inherently not parallel-safe.

Moving these attributes from class attributes to instance attributes would allow independent contexts of `LocalMachine`. Further, this gives consistency with the design of [`RemoteMachine`](https://github.com/tomerfiliba/plumbum/blob/9cfee6125c2d541ec423e1af8976da02fcc78220/plumbum/machines/remote.py#L136). Is there any negative side-effect from turning these into instance attributes?

issue_title: 获得的USER['KeyWord']不对
Windows: Icon should self-repair on explorer restart
issue_body:
When `explorer.exe` (who is responsible for drawing Notification Area) restarts (i.e. after crash) icons of most applications shown, but not for pystray. I've found that there are specific message (`TaskbarCreated`) sent in this case. There are one problem - it's only broadcasted to top-level windows, so notification-only windows (with parent HWND_MESSAGE) doesn't receive it.

Finally: I've got a PR for this.

issue_title: Only one cookie is stored if define_cassette_placeholder is used

Hi,

the following code produces a cassette that does not contain a list for every header in the recorded cassette:

```python
from betamax import Betamax
import requests

with Betamax.configure() as config:
    config.define_cassette_placeholder('<USERNAME>', 'foo')

session = requests.Session()
recorder = Betamax(session)
with recorder.use_cassette('cookie_test'):
    res = session.get('http://httpbin.org/cookies/set?k2=v2&k1=v1')
```

The cassette:

               "Server" : "nginx",
               "Content-Type" : "text/html; charset=utf-8",
               "Access-Control-Allow-Credentials" : "true",
               "Content-Length" : "223",

The result is that `Set-Cookie` is only included once, i.e. the first cookie is lost. If I remove the cassette placeholder, the cassette looks different:

issue_title: Permission issues in PyPI source tarballs
issue_body: The files inside the PyPI source tarballs are 0640, including those under `voluptuous.egg-info/`. This causes setuptools to install these metadata with 640 permission (not world readable). It would be okay to either exclude egg-info from the source tarball or make them world-readable (644).

I think that ansible_cmd is instead trying to attach the variables to any host explicitly added to the group `all`.

I have tested this with
```
inventories/
  - group_vars/
    - all
    - webservers
```
variables defined in `all` are not visible in 'Custom variables', but when moved to `webservers` they are visible.

(Note: removed file extensions from my example since they are incorrectly used, see #130)

            elif pos_tag[1].startswith('V'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.VERB)
            elif pos_tag[1].startswith('N'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.NOUN)
            elif pos_tag[1].startswith('R'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.ADV)
            else:
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.NOUN)

        # Create tokenizer and stemmer
        # tokenizer = nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize()
        lemmatizer = nltk.stem.wordnet.WordNetLemmatizer()

        def is_ci_lemma_stopword_set_match(a, b, threshold=0.5):
            """Check if a and b are matches."""
            pos_a = map(get_wordnet_pos, nltk.pos_tag(tokenizer.nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize(a)))
            pos_b = map(get_wordnet_pos, nltk.pos_tag(tokenizer.nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize(b)))
            lemmae_a = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token.lower().strip(string.punctuation), pos) for token, pos in pos_a \
                            if pos == wordnet.NOUN and token.lower().strip(string.punctuation) not in stopwords]
            lemmae_b = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token.lower().strip(string.punctuation), pos) for token, pos in pos_b \
                            if pos == wordnet.NOUN and token.lower().strip(string.punctuation) not in stopwords]

            # Calculate Jaccard similarity
            ratio = len(set(lemmae_a).intersection(lemmae_b)) / float(len(set(lemmae_a).union(lemmae_b)))
            return (ratio >= threshold)
        def get(self, input_statement):
            """
            Takes a statement string and a list of statement strings.
            Returns the approximate matching statement from the list.
            """
            statement_list = self.context.storage.get_response_statements()
            print(statement_list)
            if not statement_list:
                if self.has_storage_context:
                    # Use a randomly picked statement
                    self.logger.info(
                        u'No statements have known responses. ' +
                        u'Choosing a random response to return.'
                        )
                    return 0, self.context.storage.get_random()
                else:
                    raise self.EmptyDatasetException()

            confidence = -1
            closest_match = input_statement
            # Find the closest matching known statement
            for statement in statement_list:
                print("statement", statement.text);
                # ratio = fuzz.ratio(input_statement.text, statement.text)
                ratio = is_ci_lemma_stopword_set_match(input_statement.text, statement.text)
                if ratio > confidence:
                    confidence = ratio
                    closest_match = statement
                    # Convert the confidence integer to a percent
                confidence /= 100.0
                return confidence, closest_match

    class EmptyDatasetException(Exception):

        def __init__(self, value="An empty set was received when at least one statement was expected."):
            self.value = value

        def __str__(self):
            return repr(self.value)

```

issue_title: Logging suggestion
issue_body:
Below code in transport.py module gives out warning for every failed connection attempt in the given host:port list.

```python
except socket.error:
      self.socket = None
      connect_count += 1
      log.warning("Could not connect to host %s, port %s", host_and_port[0], host_and_port[1], exc_info=1)
```
My suggestion is to make the log statement in the above code to be logged at INFO level and give out a warning only if we fail to connect to any of the host:port pairs provided in the list.

The reason I am suggesting this is that default log level in python WARN and if the client is able to connect to one of the host:port pair in the list, It should be considered a success without any abnormalities and more often than not the warning produced by this code has caused confusions to the people not much familiar with the API.
Now, I know there are ways to suppress this log statement in the client code but they are cumbersome and making the log statement at INFO level seems like reasonable logging improvement and we can anyway log a WARNING or ERROR if we fail to connect any of host:port pair in this list.

Cheers,
Abhishek

issue_title: Implement Custom Exceptions
Linearize curves and surfaces to a tolerance
issue_body:
It looks like curvepts is made by proceeding at a sample rate of _delta from 0 to 1 proportionally along the whole length of the spline.  

For use in CAM we want the geometry subdivided only as far as necessary that the the approximation fits the the curve within the margin of a tolerance value.  Often we will set this to the tolerance of the CNC machine, but we don't want to overdo it, because the controllers tend to choke on too many close together points.

issue_title: With a default_factory and init=False, fields are initialized in the wrong order
issue_body:
The PEP doesn't say that fields are initialized in order, but I'd like to do so, anyway.

If a field has a default_factory, and has init=False, it is initialized out of order. For this code:

```
@dataclass
class C:
    x: int
    y: list = field(default_factory=list, init=False)
    z: int
```
The generated code looks like:
```
def __init__(__dataclass_self__,x:_type_x,z:_type_z)->_return_type:
 __dataclass_self__.x=x
 __dataclass_self__.z=z
 __dataclass_self__.y = _dflt_y()
```
I'll rework field initialization to make sure this is handled correctly. I think there are also likely other corner cases.

issue_title: Test on PyPy at Travis-CI
Named assignments break disambiguation
issue_body:
* parglare version: HEAD
* Python version: 3.5.2
* Operating System: linux ubuntu

### Description

Having fun trying to shoehorn a line-based language into the parser, which is somewhat asking for trouble of course. The best solution so far is to make `LAYOUT` handle only spaces and tabs, and have a dedicated `eol` non-terminal that handles line endings. That seems to mostly work except the grammar needs to handle truly empty lines explicitly (not added here for simplicity).

Anyway, while experimenting I ran into a weird disambiguation problem. See
``` python
# --------------------
gram_text = r"""
typedefheader : "define" "type"  eol ;

eol : /\n/
| filecomment
| doccomment
;

filecomment : /#.*\n/ ;
doccomment : /#<.*\n/ {20} ;

LAYOUT: EMPTY | /[ \t]+/ ;
KEYWORD: /[-a-z]+/ ;
"""
# --------------------
text = "define type #< something\n"

g = Grammar.from_string(gram_text)
p = Parser(g)
print(p.parse(text))
print("############################")
print()

# --------------------
gram_text2 = r"""
typedefheader : "define" "type"  eol ;

eol : /\n/
| filecomment
| doccomment
;

filecomment : /#.*\n/ ;
doccomment : docu=/#<.*\n/ {20} ;    // Added "docu="

LAYOUT: EMPTY | /[ \t]+/ ;
KEYWORD: /[-a-z]+/ ;
"""
# --------------------

g2 = Grammar.from_string(gram_text2)
p2 = Parser(g2)
print(p2.parse(text))
```
In the first grammar, the `eol` defines a `\n` possibly prefixed by two different forms of comment, `# filecomment` and `#< documentation comment`. Since these two forms of comment are obviously ambiguous, the second form has a nice `{20}` attached to it to give it priority. So far so good.

Now I wanted to know if it actually worked, so I added `docu=` to the `doccomment` rule (printing the result would give me a named instance rather than plain text). However, the parser then crashed on the ambiguity:
``` python console
['define', 'type', '#< something\n']
############################

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/hat/projects/psi/psi5/compiler3/parglare/parser.py", line 213, in parse
    ntok = next_token(cur_state, input_str, position)
  File "/home/hat/projects/psi/psi5/compiler3/parglare/parser.py", line 537, in _next_token
    ntok = self._lexical_disambiguation(tokens)
  File "/home/hat/projects/psi/psi5/compiler3/parglare/parser.py", line 672, in _lexical_disambiguation
    raise DisambiguationError(tokens)
parglare.exceptions.DisambiguationError: [<filecomment(#< something
)>, <#<.*\n(#< something
)>]

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "named_assignment.py", line 43, in <module>
    print(p2.parse(text))
  File "/home/hat/projects/psi/psi5/compiler3/parglare/parser.py", line 217, in parse
    disambiguation_error(e.tokens))
parglare.exceptions.ParseError: Error at position 1,12 => "fine type *#< somethi". Can't disambiguate between: <filecomment(#< something
)> or <#<.*\n(#< something
)>
```
Note the second line with hashes in the output, meaning that the first parse was fine.

issue_title: worksheet.values include_empty option bug?
issue_body:
In the `worksheet.values` function, the `include_empty` option is supposed to
to remove trailing, empty cells. But, in this example, cells are deleted which are not empty, which I believe is a bug.
    
    import pygsheets
    
    SHEET_NAME='Example'
    client = pygsheets.authorize()
    worksheet = client.create(SHEET_NAME).sheet1
    
    # Fill sheet with some example numbers
    counter = 0
    for i in range(4):
        for j in range(4):
            worksheet[i][j] = counter
            counter += 1
    
    print worksheet.values((1, 2), (3, 3), include_empty=False)
    # [ [2,3], [6,7], [10,11] ]
    print worksheet.values((1, 2), (3, 3), include_empty=True)
    # [ [2,6,10]
    # The second column of the data is deleted.

As there are no empty cells in the range specified, I don't see why the option should result in different output.

issue_title: Support for environment(env) variable format.
issue_body:
Can we have support for linux environment variable file.

Format is similar to `properties` file, just that while dumping the [file](https://github.com/ssato/python-anyconfig/blob/6450527e5691652ff2cafac94e9a39c6bcc087ab/anyconfig/backend/properties.py#L204), it should not have space on either side(Linux cannot source with spaces, it throws error). Fixing that line to 

`- stream.write("%s = %s\n" % (key, escape(val)))`
`+ stream.write("%s=%s\n" % (key, escape(val)))`

 will do it. So it can be a different backend altogether, or there could be some kind of a flag where we can specify whether it should be dumped as `env` file or `properties` file.

issue_title: `from lazy import lazy` is broken in Python 3.5
issue_body:
3.5 changed the semantics of import a little bit, one result being that the way lazy sets itself up is no longer what is expected. `from lazy import lazy` ends up with the module lazy.lazy loaded as "lazy" instead of the class within that module, and diff-cover now crashes with "TypeError: 'module' object is not callable"

I've opened a PR to fix lazy at https://github.com/stefanholek/lazy/pull/3 but the project is kind of a ghost town it seems, so I'm not sure how it should best be handled.

issue_title: Using the observer based approach randomly fails with CardRequestTimeoutException
issue_body:
I've been using the [sample_TransmitCardObserver.py](https://github.com/LudovicRousseau/pyscard/blob/aea375e4ecc8486cb810d1754f9e9668845c9343/smartcard/Examples/framework/sample_TransmitCardObserver.py) example for reading mifare-tags. I do want to run this script as a daemon, but I'm getting random `CardRequestTimeoutException: Time-out during card request` errors.
Those keep mostly popping up, when quickly interacting with the reader and the tag - putting it to the reader only for a short time and pulling it away.
The full trace is as follows:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/smartcard/CardMonitoring.py", line 160, in run
    currentcards = self.cardrequest.waitforcardevent()
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/smartcard/CardRequest.py", line 72, in waitforcardevent
    return self.pcsccardrequest.waitforcardevent()
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/smartcard/pcsc/PCSCCardRequest.py", line 309, in waitforcardevent
    raise CardRequestTimeoutException()
smartcard.Exceptions.CardRequestTimeoutException: Time-out during card request
```.
After this happens the program just hangs indefinitely.
I tracked the issue down to: https://github.com/LudovicRousseau/pyscard/pull/22
Without this PR, the Monitor just keeps on going, printing the occasional stack-trace, but otherwise seeming to work fine.
I'm now using `pyscard` exclusively with this patch locally applied. I would however love to see this fixed here. I guess for now I'd prefer for #22 to be un-merged. If the only downside of that is "duplicate error message printouts" not being prevented, I would prefer the monitoring-thread not being stopped on a `CardRequestTimeoutException`.

issue_title: Add support for defining MySQL FULLTEXT INDEX in the model
issue_body:
I originally filed this against Django (https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/26990) however it was suggested django-mysql might be a better home for now.

In MySQL 5.6 support was added to InnoDB for full text indexes:
​https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.6/en/innodb-fulltext-index.html

Currently we have to create these via a migration like:
```python
migrations.RunSQL(
    sql='CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX `idx_description` on foo(`description`);',
    reverse_sql='ALTER TABLE foo DROP INDEX idx_description',
),
```

It would be ideal to be able to define them via the model DDL instead, so the full DB state can be recreated from the model.

In the Django ticket it was mentioned this might need to wait until Django 1.11's more formal support for indexes lands:
https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/26808
https://github.com/django/django/pull/6857

Many thanks!

issue_title: Attachments have some issues with amazon s3 hosting
issue_body:
On localhost using normal local file storage upload and download of attachments works just fine.

But when uploaded files are hosted externally on Amazon S3 (external upload hosting is required on Heroku), it struggles with uploading large-ish files (for me it failed with a 10 MB file) because the upload code runs out of memory. The exact size when this happens probably depends on how beefy your server is, but it seems to be some issues with very inefficient buffer usage when it probably should just have forwarded the file directly to amazon s3; or maybe it's because it tries to read the file back to read the file size and this blows up somehow.
(My "workaround" for this was simply telling the customer not to upload large files as attachments and use the wagtail admin document upload and post links to the uploaded file instead. And limit the users who have upload permissions severely; which is sensible anyway because of malware risks)

And a related issue is when the filename contains non-ascii characters such as the norwegian letters "æøå". Then the view  for this url `{% url 'forum_conversation:attachment' pk=attachment.id %}` gives a server error.
(My workaround for this was simply bypassing the attachment view completely by linking directly to the file using `{{ attachment.file.url }}` in my template. This disables the serverside permission check, though; and makes the actual file url visible to anyone with download permissions.)

Currently using v0.5.2 but judging by the change logs of 0.5.3 and 0.5.4 i do think these issues are still valid)

I didn't save the stack-traces before fixing the issues, but if you have problems reproducing these bugs (maybe my s3 config is just stupid), I could revert my fixes on our dev-server and give you a access to the forum so you can  see the stack traces yourself.

issue_title: what is the correct way to use oauth2 with forwarded domains
issue_body:
We're using a domain-forwarded setup in which the code itself runs on `project-plus-random-prefix.ourstagingserver.org` but this code is accessed through `project-staging.ourserver.org` (which acts as router for a number of different requests, one set of those being django routes, others being completely different things). When using Google Auth (oauth2) we can't seem to point the google callback to `project-staging.ourserver.org/soc/complete/google-oauth2` because the redirect_url that the social auth library adds into the login is for  `project-plus-random-prefix.ourstagingserver.org`, and I cannot seem to find anything in the docs that explain how to tell social auth what the actual redirect uri should be.

I did find `SOCIAL_AUTH_LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL` in http://python-social-auth-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/settings.html#urls-options but this documentation seems to suggest this is a *path* rather than a full URL, so if this value can be used for a fully qualified URL, can the docs be updated to explicitly mention that? And if not, what is the correct way to make sure social-auth uses the domain that it needs to be using for auth to succeed?

issue_title: passing an existing session (with proxies that require authentication) to cloudflare-scrape
issue_body:
I am having an issue using an existing session with cloudflare-scrape. 

Code 1 works and goes through the proxy authentication just fine.

_Code 1_
```
s = requests.Session()
s.proxies.update({'http': 'http://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port', 'https': 'https://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port'})
s.trust_env=False;  #this line must be there otherwise auth wont work
res = s.get('http://python.org')
```

I tried passing that session when creating a scrapper as seen in Code 2.
_Code 2_
```
s=requests.Session()
s.proxies.update({'http': 'http://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port', 'https': 'https://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port'})
s.auth = HTTPProxyAuth('username', 'password') 
s.trust_env=False
scraper = cfscrape.create_scraper(sess=s) 
scraper.get('http://python.org').content
```

However, I got the following error, 
```
requests.exceptions.ProxyError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='python.org', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: / (Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to prox
y.', error('Tunnel connection failed: 407 Proxy Authentication Required',))).
```

Please note that I would get the same error in Code 1 if I commented out the s.trust_env=False line, which lead me to believe that the _trust_env_ variable is not populated when create_scraper is called. 
My conclusion was correct as you can see blow _trust_env_ was not one of the attributes set by create_scraper.
_original code from create_scraper_
```
 if sess:
        attrs = ["auth", "cert", "cookies", "headers", "hooks", "params", "proxies", "data"]
        for attr in attrs:
                val = getattr(sess, attr, None)
                if val:
                        setattr(scraper, attr, val)
```

I tried to simply add _trust_env_ to the attrs so that create_srcaper would set it but It did not help.
```
 if sess:
        attrs = ["trust_env","auth", "cert", "cookies", "headers", "hooks", "params", "proxies", "data"]
        for attr in attrs:
                val = getattr(sess, attr, None)
                if val:
                        setattr(scraper, attr, val)
```

issue_title: Kerberos support
No mention of libgeos requirement.
issue_body: At least in Django 1.2.3, this plugin will not run without installation of the libgeos C library. A mention of this in the documentation should be made.

issue_title: Filter fields in embedded document
issue_body:
I think this is a feature request but is there a way to filter fields from embedded documents?  Think of a base document (in mongodb) that looks like this in JSON:

```json
[{
  "_id":  12,
  "name": "test 1",
  "embdded_doc": {
     "_id": 1237652,
     "other_info": "this and that",
     "color": "red"
  } 
}]
```

and you only want to output: _id, name, embedded_doc.color

We are trying to avoid having to serialize the entire embedded doc since it could a be large memory footprint, especially when we are returning a list of the parent objects.

Thanks

issue_title: A suggestion for adding minimal support for search folders
issue_body:
Hi,

I once had to make a small addition to this excellent library to enable it to recognize search folders. I am by no means a professional Python developer so I thought I would just share a code snippet rather than making a full-fledged pull request:

```
class SearchFolders(Folder):
    DISTINGUISHED_FOLDER_ID = 'searchfolders'
    supported_item_models = ITEM_CLASSES

    LOCALIZED_NAMES = {
        'en_US': (u'Search Folders',),
        # TODO: Add more localizations
    }
```

```
searchfolders = account.root.get_default_folder(SearchFolders)
for folder in searchfolders.find_folders(shape="AllProperties"):
        print(folder.name)
```

Hopefully someone will find this useful.

issue_title: Initialization values must have unique scores, but shouldn't
issue_body: Currently, the `initialize` method requires a dictionary of values and positions to be passed where the values are used as keys. This means that any two positions that share the same value cannot be passed to the routine, as the keys will clash. Internally it appears there is nothing that prevents this change from happening as the positions/values are eventually stored as arrays anyway. Ideally, initialize should accept an array of dictionaries instead, such that positions that share the same value do not clash.

issue_title: Defining an __eq__ method on LoLException caused it to become unhashable
issue_body:
The biggest problem w/this for me -- although I imagine it could apply to a lot of other users -- is illustrated by the following example:

```
import logging
from riorwatcher.riotwatcher import RiotWatcher, LoLException

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
rw = RiotWatcher(api_key)

try:
    result = rw.get_summoner(name='foo', region='bar')
except LoLException as e:
    logger.exception('something happen')
```

The `exception` method of python logger objects automatically includes the raised exception in output.

However, LoLException is unhashable, which causes a `Logging Error` to occur.

I have never run into an issue like this before, but a cursory googling reveals the following solution:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1608842/types-that-define-eq-are-unhashable-in-python-3-x

Sure enough, removing the `__eq__` method from LoLException makes the exception loggable via `logger.exception`. To paraphrase the SO answer, if you wish to keep `__eq__` the solution seems like simply defining `__hash__`.

_As an aside, this is also problematic for task queues that must work w/raised exceptions during the processing of jobs, e.g. to retry a task that failed due to a rate limit exceeded error, etc._

issue_title: OpenSSL issue and possible use of urandom
issue_body:
Hi @James1345 and other contributors, first thanks for the great app!

I use Knox in production on several projects. This time I need to deploy one of those in a Linux shared server in a hosting provider (WebFaction). The problem is when I login I always receive the same token no matter the user credentials I send. Naturally, when trying to login a second time I get:

```
"(1062, "Duplicate entry '1080f871e39cc839e5bcc9f852f9a8f3ddc03cf7e72e9fd1d6e4a71d7e74936f' for key 'PRIMARY'")".
```

I noticed the problem is with the random bytes generation. When Knox tries to generate salt and token it only gets a bunch of zeros from `OpenSSL.rand.bytes`. So when it uses the zeros to digest the token, the result is always the same. This generates a database error since token field is, of course, unique.

As I needed to deploy the project fast I started to mess around with Knox code and found that if I replace the `OpenSSL.rand.bytes` with `os.urandom` it solves the problem. Is also good to mention both `os.urandom` and `OpenSSL.rand.bytes` work fine in the Python terminal of the same server.

In the documentation of Knox is said that "DRF Tokens are generated with os.urandom, which is not cryptographically secure. Knox uses OpenSSL to provide tokens." But the documentation of [pyOpenSSL rand](http://www.pyopenssl.org/en/stable/api/rand.html) module says "Functions from this module shouldn’t be used. Use urandom instead." pointing the [this](https://sockpuppet.org/blog/2014/02/25/safely-generate-random-numbers/) article.

So could some one clarify if is it really worse to use `os.random` and why?

issue_title: Custom folder for local Cache DB
issue_body:
I did a small tweak so we can have custom folder for local cache DB.

`class RandomUserAgentMiddleware(object):
    def __init__(self, crawler):
        super(RandomUserAgentMiddleware, self).__init__()

        loc = crawler.settings.get('LOCATION')
        if loc is None: 
            self.ua = UserAgent()		    
        else:
            self.ua = UserAgent(path=crawler.settings.get('LOCATION'))        
        self.per_proxy = crawler.settings.get('RANDOM_UA_PER_PROXY', False)        
        self.proxy2ua = {}`

In settings file, u can have an option like:
LOCATION = '/home/user/fake_useragent%s.json' % fake_useragent.VERSION

issue_title: Allowing More Than One Subscriber?
issue_body:
I want to use django-websocket-redis for more than one type of app. I touched on this the other day, but didn't really collect my thoughts enough and thus [removed the issue](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/issues/167).

I think in the simplest form, I want to have different subscribers for different facilities. Much like Django's urls.py in how a regex points to a view. For example, we could define WS4REDIS_SUBSCRIBERS like below, where the key is a regex matched with the request's facility string.  Then the WsgiServer would map the request to the correct Subscriber class.

```python
WS4REDIS_SUBSCRIBERS = {
    'someapp': 'mysite.subscribers.SomeAppSubscriber',
    'chat-(.*?)': 'mysite.subscribers.ChatSubscriber',
    '...',
}
```

issue_title: Use websocket-client to create a connection, why the runserver way to start the Django will automatically restart
issue_body:
Hi, 
I use `python2.7 manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8234` to start examples, and then use websocket-client to create a connection:
```
from websocket import create_connection
def test_pubsub_broadcast():
    websocket_url = 'ws://192.168.2.214:8234/ws/foobar?subscribe-broadcast&publish-broadcast&echo'
    ws = create_connection(websocket_url)
```
Connection can be created successfully, but runserver will immediately restart, if you continue to use this connection to send data will be reported to the wrong:
```
[[Errno 111] Connection refused]
```

issue_title: ManyRelatedField support
issue_body:
Thanks for the package, although I've spent quite some time to make it work with ManyRelatedField, even though the solution was quite simple.

The reason I use ManyRelatedField is because ListField raises `'RelatedManager' is not iterable`, which makes sense.

My solution is:
    
    class RecursiveManyRelatedField(serializers.ManyRelatedField, serializers.BaseSerializer):
        pass

    class CommentSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
        children = RecursiveManyRelatedField(child_relation=RecursiveField(to='CommentSerializer', allow_null=True), read_only=True)


to avoid:

    assert issubclass(parent_class, BaseSerializer)   #line 83 of fileds.py

Works like a charm, just thought it should be supported. Thank you.

issue_title: IndexError in get_batch_result_iter
issue_body:
Happens when trying to **update the results from an upsert** job.

Seems like the second request to fetch the result data is not necessary, since the response data from the first one already contains the _final results_ with each upserted object, instead of the _intermediate one_ with the results ids.

So when we try to extract the result id in line 505 (`result_id = r.text.split("<result>")[1].split("</result>")[0]`) it blows up, since there is no xml tag at all.

Here's an example I've just ran:

```
uri = self.endpoint + "/job/%s/batch/%s/result" % (job_id, batch_id)
r = requests.get(uri, headers=self.headers(), stream=True)
print(r.text)
>>> u'"Id","Success","Created","Error"\n"701o0000000kiShAAI","true","true",""\n'
```

I assume this behaviour is not observed with other kinds of jobs, so in that case I guess we could check whether the text contains the ids or the full result set.

issue_title: parse_x_forwarded_for uses wrong IP from X-Forwarded-For
issue_body:
Currently Daphne uses the right-most IP when parsing `X-Forwarded-For` while the original client would be the left-most IP.

See: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-Forwarded-For#Format) and [Twisted implementation](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/e920939343ac5f6795c4509e0fe25c33a2447531/src/twisted/web/http.py#L2361-L2362).

issue_title: SQS too slow
issue_body:
It takes more time to push and pop from the SQS queue than to actually generate the layers.

I did a small piece of code to compare SQS with Redis (on another machine) and I've got those results:
```
push_redis(): 1228.101962us/op
stats_redis(): 1159.500808us/op
pop_redis(): 1319.037752us/op

push_sqs(): 48042.700638us/op
stats_sqs(): 8742.319578us/op
pop_sqs(): 15600.274294us/op
```

The code:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import boto.sqs
import boto.sqs.jsonmessage
import pickle
import redis
import timeit

NUMBER = 500

sqs_queue = None
redis_con = None
counter = 0

def connect_sqs():
    global sqs_queue
    connection = boto.sqs.connect_to_region('eu-west-1')
    sqs_queue = connection.get_queue('XXXXXXXXXX')
    sqs_queue.set_message_class(boto.sqs.jsonmessage.JSONMessage)


def push_sqs():
    global sqs_queue, counter
    counter += 1
    sqs_message = sqs_queue.new_message()
    sqs_message['z'] = 0
    sqs_message['x'] = 1
    sqs_message['y'] = counter
    sqs_message['n'] = 3
    sqs_message['metadata'] = {'layer': 'toto'}

    sqs_queue.write(sqs_message)


def stats_sqs():
    global sqs_queue
    sqs_queue.get_attributes()


def pop_sqs():
    global sqs_queue
    sqs_message = sqs_queue.read()
    assert sqs_message is not None
    # print(sqs_message.get('y'))
    sqs_message.delete()


def connect_redis():
    global redis_con
    redis_con = redis.StrictRedis(host="redis", port=6379)


def push_redis():
    global redis_con, counter
    counter += 1
    message = dict(z=0, x=1, y=counter, n=3, metadata={'layer': 'toto'})
    redis_con.lpush('test_queue', pickle.dumps(message))


def stats_redis():
    global redis_con
    redis_con.llen('test_queue')


def pop_redis():
    global redis_con
    _queue, message = redis_con.brpop('test_queue')
    decoded = pickle.loads(message)
    # print(repr(decoded))


def time_it(stmt):
    measure = timeit.timeit(stmt=stmt, number=NUMBER, globals=globals())
    print("%s: %fus/op" % (stmt, measure/NUMBER*1000000))


def main():
    global redis_con, sqs_queue
    connect_redis()
    time_it('push_redis()')
    time_it('stats_redis()')
    time_it('pop_redis()')
    print("Redis queue length: " + repr(redis_con.llen('test_queue')))

    connect_sqs()
    time_it('push_sqs()')
    time_it('stats_sqs()')
    time_it('pop_sqs()')
    print("SQS queue attrs: " + repr(sqs_queue.get_attributes()))


main()
```

issue_title: Use GAE cursors on datastore
issue_body:
It looks like Datastore queries are limited to 1000 items .
If you want to send a mail to each of your 50000 users, you can do somethink like this on native GAE :

```
....
if cursor != '':
Q = db.Query(models.Partner).with_cursor(cursor)
else:
Q = db.Query(models.Partner)
....
for partner in Q.fetch(10):
mail.send_mail(....)
cursor = Q.cursor()
taskqueue.add(url='/mailworker/', params={'cursor':cursor})
```

It would be nice if Pydal provides a way to either:

- do the same, but only with the DAL interface,
- use native GAE cursor upon DAL objects (I suspect this is already possible, but it not clear to me how to do this)

issue_title: DAL initialization performance and reuse
issue_body:
Hi!

I'm using pydal in an app backend with about 50 table definitions (`define_table`). The DAL object is created for each HTTP request, and discarded after the request ends.

The DAL(...) initialization time takes between 30-40ms (with disabled migrations), which is very long to construct the static database definition. This limits the max req/s to ~25. The db schema also doesn't change between requests, so I think it's wasted time. There are also a lot of checks and assertions in the `define_table()` and `Table()` constructor, which must not be executed for each request.

What I was first thinking of is to create the table definitions only the first time (during app startup), and to reuse the table definitions for all requests. This is somehow difficult, because each Table object has a reference to the DAL object. I would have to turn all references to the new created DAL object. No idea if this can work at all.

Instead I tried to not destroy the DAL objects but to store it in a queue after the request, and reuse it for the next request:

```python
queue = gevent.queue.Queue()

def request():
    if not queue.qsize():
        queue.put_nowait(create_dal(db_uri))

    db = queue.get_nowait()
    db._adapter.reconnect()

    try:
        # handle the request
        handle_request(db)
        db.commit()
        queue.put(db)
    except:
        db.rollback()
```

The `db._adapter.reconnect()` assigns the current thread a connection, and tests if the db connection is still alive. If the request raises an error, I do not reuse the DAL object.

The first PoC in my app backend shows that this pattern seems to work. Does anybody know if the reuse of the DAL objects can have any side effects I am currently not aware of?
